note: Neutral context markers for the historical timeline. Each describes what happened, not who was to blame.

events.title: Napoleonic Wars end
events.summary: Decades of war left government debt above twice the size of the economy — among the highest ratios in British history.

events.title: First World War ends
events.summary: Borrowing to fund the war pushed debt sharply higher, reshaping public finances for a generation.

events.title: Second World War ends
events.summary: Debt peaked at well over 200% of GDP. It then fell over several decades as the economy grew.

events.title: 1976 IMF loan
events.summary: Facing a sterling crisis, the UK borrowed from the International Monetary Fund — a notable moment in 1970s economic difficulty.

events.title: Global financial crisis
events.summary: A banking crisis and recession raised borrowing and debt as tax revenue fell and support measures were funded.

events.title: COVID-19 pandemic
events.summary: Emergency support for households and businesses, alongside a fall in the economy, pushed borrowing to peacetime records.

events.title: Interest rates rise
events.summary: Higher inflation and interest rates increased the cost of servicing debt, including index-linked gilts.
